papers.title: Melting, Processing, and Properties of Disordered Fe-Al and Fe-Al-C Based Alloys
papers.paper_scope: Development of cost-effective melting and processing routes for disordered Fe-7Al and Fe-9Al lightweight steels, evaluation of microstructure and mechanical properties, and study of carbon addition (0.002–1.0 wt.%) on Fe-7Al alloy.

alloys.alloys_notes: Measured AIMFC composition: Al 7.30, Si 0.14, Mn 0.045, S 0.0016, C 0.019, O 0.0057, H 0.0023, N 0.0018 wt.%. Measured VIM composition: Al 7.02, Si 0.12, Mn 0.052, S 0.0028, C 0.015, O 0.0035, H 0.0020, N <0.001 wt.%.

Measured AIMFC composition: Al 9.20, Si 0.16, Mn 0.046, S 0.0015, C 0.017, O 0.0050, H 0.0052, N 0.0015 wt.%. Measured VIM composition: Al 9.10, Si 0.11, Mn 0.042, S 0.0027, C 0.014, O 0.0023, H 0.0021, N <0.001 wt.%.
Measured composition: Al 6.86, Si 0.18, Mn 0.16, S 0.004, C 0.002 wt.%.
Measured composition: Al 6.92, Si 0.17, Mn 0.18, S 0.005, C 0.15 wt.%.
Measured composition: Al 6.78, Si 0.19, Mn 0.15, S 0.004, C 0.35 wt.%.

processes.description: Air induction melting (AIM) without flux cover, followed by hot forging and hot rolling to 12 mm plate.
processes.processes_notes: As-cast ingots exhibited gas porosity and higher hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur levels.

processes.description: Air induction melting with proprietary flux cover (Al2O3-CaO-MgO-CaF2) and charging schedule, followed by hot forging and hot rolling to 12 mm plate.
processes.processes_notes: Produced sound, porosity-free ingots with low sulfur and oxygen comparable to VIM.

processes.description: Vacuum induction melting (VIM) at 10^-4 torr with alumina-lined crucible, poured at 1893 K, followed by hot forging and hot rolling to 12 mm plate.
processes.processes_notes: Very low hydrogen and impurity levels; excellent Al recovery (96–98%).

processes.description: Air induction melting with flux cover for Fe-9Al, followed by hot forging and hot rolling to 12 mm plate.
processes.processes_notes: Ingots free from gas porosity but exhibited microcracks due to higher residual hydrogen; poor hot workability.

processes.description: Vacuum induction melting for Fe-9Al, followed by hot forging and hot rolling to 12 mm plate.
processes.processes_notes: Sound ingots with excellent hot workability and low hydrogen.

processes.description: Air induction melting with flux cover for Fe-7Al-C alloys with varying carbon (0.002–1.0 wt.%), followed by hot forging and hot rolling to 12 mm plate.
processes.processes_notes: Used to study effect of carbon on microstructure and mechanical properties.

processes.description: Air induction melting with flux cover for Fe-7Al, followed by hot forging, hot rolling, cold rolling, and annealing to 2 mm sheet.
processes.processes_notes: Cold-rolled and annealed sheet exhibited excellent room-temperature ductility (about 40% elongation).

characterization_methods.purpose: microstructural examination of carbon-containing Fe-7Al alloys
characterization_methods.key_findings: Single-phase structure with no precipitates in 0.002C alloy; precipitates appear at higher carbon levels.

characterization_methods.purpose: phase identification of precipitates in carbon-containing Fe-7Al alloys
characterization_methods.key_findings: Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates confirmed by XRD using Cu-Ka radiation on Philips 3710 diffractometer; similar peaks observed in 0.35–1.0 wt.% C alloys.
Average of five measurements; hardness values reported in HV scale.
Average of three tests; UTS, YS, and total elongation measured.

characterization_methods.purpose: measure hydrogen content in as-cast ingots
characterization_methods.key_findings: Hydrogen levels: AIM Fe-7Al 0.0052 wt.%, AIMFC Fe-7Al 0.0023 wt.%, VIM Fe-7Al 0.0020 wt.%, AIMFC Fe-9Al 0.0052 wt.%, VIM Fe-9Al 0.0021 wt.%.

unmapped_findings: Fe-Al alloys containing 6–9 wt.% aluminum exhibit a density reduction of approximately 10% compared with conventional steels, offering high specific elastic stiffness and strength.
Fe-9Al alloy ingots produced by AIM exhibited extensive gas porosity and could not be thermomechanically processed or characterized for mechanical properties.
Aluminum recovery during AIMFC and VIM is 95–98%, which is superior to AIM and comparable between AIMFC and VIM.
AIMFC ingots exhibit lower sulfur content than AIM and VIM ingots due to desulfurization by CaO present in the flux cover via slag–metal reactions.
Table V caption and Fig. 5 caption refer to the carbon-series alloys as 'AIM', whereas the main text explicitly states these alloys were prepared by air induction melting with a flux cover (AIMFC).
Fe-9Al alloy is expected to exhibit short-range ordering, whereas Fe-7Al is a totally disordered solid solution.
Cold-rolled and annealed Fe-7Al AIMFC sheet (2 mm thickness) exhibited excellent room-temperature ductility of about 40%, compared with 18% for the hot-rolled AIMFC condition.
The poor room-temperature ductility of Fe-7Al produced by AIM is attributed to hydrogen embrittlement and higher oxygen/sulfur impurities leading to nonmetallic inclusions.
The poor ductility of Fe-9Al AIMFC (0.1% elongation) is attributed to microcracks from residual hydrogen, whereas VIM Fe-9Al shows good ductility (9% elongation) and hot workability.

Increasing carbon content in Fe-7Al alloy from 0.002 to 1.0 wt.% resulted in significant improvement in room-temperature strength (YS 382–891 MPa, UTS 425–1120 MPa) but adversely affected ductility (EL 21–1.5%) due to increasing volume fraction of Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates.
